text.title: SLO Definition Template Per Service Class Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the SLO Definition Template Per Service Class methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Each service-class MUST have a canonical SLI template (PromQL query + label keys + window). Authors fork the template; they don't write SLIs from scratch.
rule.rationale: Hand-rolled SLIs drift in format; templates make them comparable across teams.

rule.statement: User-facing SLIs MUST measure the customer experience: HTTP success rate, end-to-end latency, error rate. Resource-utilisation metrics (CPU, RAM) are NOT user-facing SLIs.
rule.rationale: CPU < 80% can hold while customers see 5xx; SLIs measure the customer, not the box.

rule.statement: Every SLO target carries a recorded rationale referencing customer commitment, business cost of breach, or peer benchmark. '99.9% because it sounds good' is rejected.

rationale.source: SLO design — Liu, Beyer et al.

rule.rationale: Magic numbers calcify; documented rationale lets future authors recalibrate.

rule.statement: Error-budget window MUST match the decision cycle: 30 days for ship-freeze decisions, 7 days for canary-halt decisions, 24h for incident bridge decisions.
rule.rationale: 30-day windows hide fast-burn; 1h windows are too noisy for ship-freeze. Window must match the action it gates.

rule.statement: SLI PromQL MUST be a recording rule (level:metric:operation pattern), not an ad-hoc query in alert/dashboard. Recording rule is the canonical SLI series.
rule.rationale: Ad-hoc queries drift; one recording rule per SLI prevents 3 dashboards measuring 'availability' differently.

rule.statement: Every service in the catalog MUST be assigned to exactly one service class; services without a class are flagged as gaps, not silently skipped.
rule.rationale: Unclassified services produce orphan SLOs or none at all.
